radicals.meaningMnemonic: You shoot your gun at the sun in winter, making it implode and turn into a <radical>black hole</radical>. Great, now it's permanent winter and the black hole is sucking everything on Earth into it — including your gun and some unlucky skier who's having the ride of their life around the edge of the black hole.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> lets you <kanji>add</kanji> words together to form a sentence. Marvel at the mighty additive power your mouth holds! You add words together in an amazing fashion!
kanji.meaningHint: Say this sentence out loud. Incredible! You've used the power of your mouth to add words together in a sentence. Your parents must be very proud of you.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Your parents are so proud of you being able to <kanji>add</kanji> words together that they surprise you with a new <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Wow! You add some stickers to your bumper and add your favorite playlist to the car radio. Let's drive.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine riding around in your new car, singing songs with your powerful mouth to the playlists you added to the fancy new radio.

kanji.meaningMnemonic:
On the bottom of <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>root</radical>s, there’s a <radical>stamp</radical> that tells you the <kanji>season</kanji> in which it was cut.

Additionally, the stamp also has information on the condition of the bamboo’s <kanji>joint</kanji>s, because no one wants to buy bamboo with damaged joints!
kanji.meaningHint: Picture yourself in a store, looking at a bunch of different bamboo stamps to make sure you don’t buy one that’s out of season, or one that has damaged joints. The season and the joints, that’s what quality bamboo is all about, and it’s why we have these stamps!
kanji.readingMnemonic: These <kanji>season</kanji> stamps are marked on bamboo <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). You can't buy a single banana or a single grape, and just like that, if you want some nice seasonal bamboo, you have to buy them in sets.
kanji.readingHint: How many bamboo sets will you be buying this season? I'm getting 42 sets.

rawData.reading_hint: How many bamboo sets will you be buying this season? I'm getting 42 sets.

Additionally, the stamp also has information on the condition of the bamboo’s <kanji>joint</kanji>s, because no one wants to buy bamboo with damaged joints!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Direct a <radical>tsunami</radical> into a <radical>canine</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> to <kanji>decrease</kanji> the amount of water that strikes the surrounding area.
kanji.meaningHint: Just make sure you get a really thirsty canine, because they're going to need to drink a LOT of water to decrease the tsunami's power.
kanji.readingMnemonic: After you and your canine <kanji>decrease</kanji> the tsunami's power, <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) comes out to thank you. That tsunami was on track to wash away Genji's house, which would have forced him to majorly decrease his frivolous playboy activities.
kanji.readingHint: Now you're wondering if you made the right choice. The world might actually be a better place if Genji decreased his silly lover-boy antics a bit.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: When they migrate to a <radical>river</radical>, a group of <radical>geoducks</radical> must do so in a certain <kanji>order</kanji> and <kanji>sequence</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Geoducks usually live in the ocean, but when they migrate to a river, they do so in a specific, predetermined order. Imagine what that sequence might be. Maybe in order of neck length, with the longest-necked geoducks going first? Or maybe in alphabetical order?
kanji.readingMnemonic: Actually, they go in birth-month <kanji>order</kanji>, starting from <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). No one knows why they follow this June-based <kanji>sequence</kanji>. But the geoducks born in June always go first in the migration order.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine it's June, and you're on a beach somewhere watching a bunch of geoducks make their way to a river. Marvel at the biological clock that determines the June-based migration sequence for these fascinating creatures.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you put a <radical>roof</radical> over a <radical>valley</radical> it will change its <kanji>form</kanji>. Its <kanji>appearance</kanji> will alter completely!
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a valley in your mind. Now put a roof on it. Hmm. It isn't <kanji>shape</kanji>d like a valley anymore.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Its new <kanji>appearance</kanji> looks familiar to you, like it's a big <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう) cup! A valley with a roof on it looks exactly like a yogurt cup. Huh!
kanji.readingHint: Now imagine this taking shape before your eyes and becoming actual yogurt! You grab a giant spoon and take a bite.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: To a <radical>narwhal</radical>, a <radical>towel</radical> is just normal <kanji>cloth</kanji>. They're always wet so towel cloth is all they know.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a narwhal wrapped in a fuzzy towel blanket, with a towel tail cover, and some towel underpants. Everything is made of this type of cloth.
kanji.readingMnemonic: If you use this type of <kanji>cloth</kanji> for your clothes and blankets people will call you a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). They think you're a fool who can't tell the difference between human cloth and towel cloth.
kanji.readingHint: "You fool, you aren't a narwhal, that cloth isn't for you!" they say. They're so mean.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Give some <radical>shellfish</radical> to a <radical>talent</radical>ed person and they'll have no problem accumulating <kanji>wealth</kanji>. People with talent can make money out of pretty much anything, so shellfish are like easy-mode.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture the talented person huddled over a pile of shellfish, whispering to them about the wealth they'll soon be turned into.
kanji.readingMnemonic: This talented person really starts accumulating <kanji>wealth</kanji> when their <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) is unveiled. It's a half-man, half-shellfish cyborg that plays the <reading>xy</reading>lophone  (ざい), and everyone wants to buy it.
kanji.readingHint: You'd be wealthy too if you sold that cyborg you made. It may not have any shellfish in it and it can't play the xylophone, but you could change that…

kanji.meaningMnemonic: There are <radical>flowers</radical> on your <radical>right</radical> to show that you're still <kanji>young</kanji>. Your body still works, you're happy, you have your entire life ahead of you! And everything smells like flowers.
kanji.meaningHint: Look to your right, if you see flowers (metaphorically, maybe) then you're still young. Congratulations!
kanji.readingMnemonic: When <kanji>young</kanji> people look to the right, they'll see their future: a <reading>walke</reading>r (わか). You know, those metal contraptions with tennis balls on the bottoms to make sure they don't scrape the floor. Everything may be flowers and happiness now, but your future is in a walker.
kanji.readingHint: All young people have a walker looming just off to the side. A sign that your youth won't last forever, dear summer child.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> to the <radical>director</radical> is judged immediately. You're having trouble understanding this <kanji>part of speech</kanji>, so he's trying to help you. But you say it over and over and he keeps saying you're wrong.
kanji.meaningHint:
"Is it an adjective?"
"No, that's the wrong part of speech."
"Is it a verb?"
"N-no! Of course not!"
kanji.readingMnemonic: The director asks you again, "What is the part of speech for the word <reading>shee</reading>p (し)?"
kanji.readingHint: You stare blankly at him. What part of speech is that?! It's an animal, you know that. Oh! A noun. Duh.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If the <radical>sun</radical> is <radical>compare</radical>d to an <kanji>insect</kanji>, you’ll see that they're both shiny, but that the sun is much shinier than the insect.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine holding up an insect to the sun to compare the two. See how they both shine, but the sun shines more brightly than the insect.
kanji.readingMnemonic: While you’re holding up an <kanji>insect</kanji> to the sun for comparison, a flock of <reading>con</reading>dors (こん) flies by, blocking out the sun. There are so many condors that they manage to totally block out the sun.
kanji.readingHint: Feel how dark and cold it is suddenly with all those condors blocking out the sun.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Passed out in front of your <radical>gate</radical> lies a former <radical>leader</radical>. He's now a <radical>drunkard</radical>, but once ran a popular <kanji>clique</kanji> called the Cool Kids <kanji>Clan</kanji>. The clan has fallen apart, and he no longer has a clique to call his own.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine this washed up drunkard leader passed out in front of the gate to your house. You have to pass him whenever you enter or exit your house. He's gross and he smells. Experience the stench in your nostrils. How he was ever the leader of a clan you have no idea.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Eventually you get sick and tired of this washed up <kanji>clan</kanji> leader and you gather your own <kanji>clique</kanji> to chase this guy away from your gate and your property. You and your friends grab some <reading>bats</reading> (ばつ) and use these bats to beat the drunkard leader. Being beaten with bats frightens him, and he scurries away.
kanji.readingHint: Hear the crushing blows of bats against flesh as you imagine this scene in your mind. You're beating the crap out of this guy with a bunch of bats, but hey — he had it coming.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: At the edge of the <radical>cliff</radical>, two <radical>tree</radical>s mark the spot where you must <radical>stop</radical>, since any <kanji>continuation</kanji> requires a way across. The trees seem long enough to reach to the other side, but how are you going to chop them down?
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself on this cliff. You desire a continuation for your journey, not wanting to stop here. Feel the strong wind on your body, blowing your hair about as you stand on the edge of the cliff. The leaves on the trees rustle in the breeze.
kanji.readingMnemonic: <kanji>Continuation</kanji> is impossible unless you chop these trees down to make a bridge to the other side. You decide to use a <reading>wrecki</reading>ng (れき) ball. Luckily, there's one right by you. So you take the wrecking ball and knock the trees over, making a bridge with them.
kanji.readingHint: Picture yourself using the wrecking ball to knock down the two trees on the cliff. Hear the the trees crack and splinter from the force of the wrecking ball. That wrecking ball is the sound of continuation.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>bookshelf</radical> as a <kanji>book counter</kanji>. Duh. What, are you gonna count them one by one? Okay, well maybe, but you should count them while they're nice and organized on the shelves. Otherwise counting is going to be a huge pain.

kanji.readingHint: Imagine how tedious it'd be to watch her counting one by one. Teach her your book counter ways!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>roof</radical> to keep yourself <radical>dry</radical> and safe from <kanji>outer space</kanji>. Look outside, above your roof, and you'll see outer space stretching above you.
kanji.meaningHint: You don't want to float up into outer space and get all wet, do you? No? Then you better make sure you have a roof above your head.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Why isn't it dry in <kanji>outer space</kanji>, you ask? Because of all the <reading>u</reading>ni (う) swimming around up there. They have nasty, wet, pointy spikes and they're all over the place.
kanji.readingHint: Look closely up at outer space and you'll see all of the uni floating around up there. They're really dark, so it can be hard to notice them, but I promise you, they're there.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You jumped off your <radical>roof</radical> for some <radical>reason</radical> and now you're floating in <kanji>midair</kanji>! You kick your legs a bit and move your arms like you're in water, but you're still just stuck there in midair.
kanji.meaningHint: At the moment you can't remember the reason you did it, but when you jumped off your roof you just floated in midair.
kanji.readingMnemonic: As you spin a bit in <kanji>midair</kanji> you look up and see <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう) looking down at you from a spaceship. He's laughing his Chewbacca laugh and you realize he has you trapped in his tractor beam.
kanji.readingHint: Oh Chewbacca, you lovable fuzzball. You better not drop me in midair, or I'll die. I hope you bring me up there!

rawData.meaning_hint: At the moment you can't remember the reason you did it, but when you jumped off your roof you just floated in midair.

rawData.meaning_mnemonic: You jumped off your <radical>roof</radical> for some <radical>reason</radical> and now you're floating in <kanji>midair</kanji>! You kick your legs a bit and move your arms like you're in water, but you're still just stuck there in midair.
rawData.reading_mnemonic: As you spin a bit in <kanji>midair</kanji> you look up and see <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう) looking down at you from a spaceship. He's laughing his Chewbacca laugh and you realize he has you trapped in his tractor beam.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You'll work your <radical>soul</radical> to <radical>death</radical> if you're too <kanji>busy</kanji>. Being busy just isn't good for your soul. Take breaks. Go on vacation. Don't be so busy that you destroy yourself.
kanji.meaningHint: Look around you. See all those busy people? Their souls are dying. Don't end up like them.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Take time out of your <kanji>busy</kanji> life and go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) once in a while. In fact, you should probably go bowling once a week at least. Nothing brings energy back to your life like some relaxing bowling.
kanji.readingHint: Think about your busy schedule this week. Now cancel everything and just go bowling instead.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: This <radical>flag</radical> marks where people used to <radical>loiter</radical> until a <radical>black hole</radical> swallowed them up, leaving behind only their <kanji>footwear</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine coming across this flag that memorializes the loiterers. You hear it flapping in the wind and wonder: Why did a black hole appear so suddenly here? Those poor, poor loiterers. Sucked right out of their footwear.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you look closer, it turns out the loiterers had all been wearing <kanji>footwear</kanji> made of <reading>ree</reading>ds (り). Makes sense, since a loiterer's laidback lifestyle is perfectly suited for reed footwear.
kanji.readingHint: Now take this rough, reed footwear in your hands. Caress it as if trying to conjure a genie out of a lamp — though your only wish is to know what happened here.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>mouth</radical> to take a <radical>measurement</radical> of this <kanji>group</kanji>. Just stretch your mouth out and around every single person. Get 'em all in there.
kanji.meaningHint: I hope this group is full of people who enjoy being inside someone else's mouth, because they're gonna be pretty mad when you let them out if they aren't.
kanji.readingMnemonic: This <kanji>group</kanji> is so big it takes you till <reading>dawn</reading> (だん) to get your measurement: they weigh a <reading>ton</reading> (とん) too!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine letting this group of, now very wet, people out of your mouth one by one. They don't seem to care that they weigh a ton! That's a large measurement, they should be proud.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> gets <radical>together</radical> with <radical>water</radical>, only <kanji>violence</kanji> can ensue. They don’t mix well together. The sun loves to fight water and water loves to fight the sun. Lots and lots of violence.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this violent fight scene in your brain. Imagine water locked in battle with the sun as the sun’s fire flares up and the water starts to sizzle.
kanji.readingMnemonic: But after a while, the sun and the water have had enough of <kanji>violence</kanji>. They decide to settle their differences with a friendly game of <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). Bowling is a great way to relieve stress and in the end, they bond over it.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine the sun and the water trying to bowl. Hear the sound of bowling balls rolling down the lane and pins rattling around and crashing together.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A raging <radical>tsunami</radical> rises next to the calm <radical>sun</radical>. When you try to <radical>compare</radical> these two things, it's clear that they cannot <kanji>mix</kanji>. If a tsunami is coming, it will likely get in the way of the sun.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine the sun getting blocked out as a tsunami rises up from the ocean. Feel the cold fear of death on you as the tsunami approaches. That tsunami is gonna mix you up.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Just before the tsunami crashes over you, a <reading>con</reading>dor (こん) swoops out of the sky and grabs you up. This condor must be a <kanji>mix</kanji> — a normal condor is not strong enough to pick up a human. The condor must be mixed with something else. Like a pterodactyl.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself getting mixed up in this situation and the pterodactyl-sized condor picking you up. Feel the condor's talons digging into your shoulders as it grabs you up and flies you towards the sun.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you stab somebody in the <radical>tongue</radical> with an <radical>umbrella</radical>, it’s sure to start a <kanji>riot</kanji> and cause <kanji>disorder</kanji>. Nobody likes to get stabbed in the tongue with an umbrella, so they’re probably going to retaliate, and before you know it, you’ve a full scale riot on your hands. Look what you’ve done.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself getting stabbed in the tongue with an umbrella. It’s very painful. Taste the blood and metal in your mouth as this happens to you.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Just now you realize that the man you stabbed in the tongue with your umbrella was none other than Macho Man <reading>Ran</reading>dy Savage (らん). Being a professional wrestler, Macho Man Randy Savage is ready to throw down at a moment’s notice. A <kanji>riot</kanji> immediately erupts. It’s a battle royal with you and Randy at the epicenter.
kanji.readingHint: Picture yourself squaring off with Macho Man Randy Savage in the middle of this riot. You have the advantage of having an umbrella, but Randy has the advantage of being himself. Picture yourself getting into a fight with him.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: “Don’t <radical>loiter</radical> – <radical>run</radical>, <kanji>Junior</kanji>!” said Indiana Jones Senior to Indiana Jones Junior. They’re escaping from a sticky situation and Junior was just loitering around like a fool. It’s no time to loiter, it’s time to run!
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine Sean Connery saying “Don’t loiter – run, Junior!” and you should have a much easier time remembering this phrase if you remember it in his voice.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Deciding not to loiter any longer, Indiana Jones <kanji>Junior</kanji> takes his dad’s advice and starts to run. Unfortunately he wasn’t paying attention to where his feet were and he stubbed his <reading>to</reading>e (と) on a jagged rock. His toe is in so much pain that he can’t bear to run any longer.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine that you are Indiana Jones Junior and this is happening to you. Feel the terrible pain of your throbbing toe as you clutch it, with the bad guys catching up with you.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around the <radical>sun</radical> <radical>temple</radical>, it's likely you'll <kanji>acquire</kanji> something. When you loiter around like that it looks like you're waiting for something, so if someone puts something in your hands and you just happen to acquire it, well, no one can fault you for that.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine loitering around near the sun temple. Maybe you acquire some illegal goods, maybe you just acquire a tan (it is a SUN temple, after all).
kanji.readingMnemonic: You managed to <kanji>acquire</kanji> some tobacco, but just as you're about to leave <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu comes marching up to you!! Or at least, his ghost does. He stares you dead in the eyes and says, "I didn't found the shogunate for you to acquire illegal goods and kill your body toking on my soil."
kanji.readingHint: Your eyes fill with tears under Tokugawa's gaze and you drop everything you acquired to the ground. You bow before him apologizing. You'll never do this again!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: It's important to focus <radical>oneself</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> so that one can emerge <kanji>renew</kanji>ed! You can't really go outside in winter without getting cold or sick, so spend winter focusing on YOU.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine sitting in your house all winter, meditating and thinking about how you can renew yourself.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Winter ends and, <kanji>renew</kanji>ed, you come out ready to start a new hobby: <reading>kay</reading>aking (かい)! Nothing says renewal like a brand new kayak and sense of purpose in life.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine being back in your house in winter. You're thinking about all the kayaking you're gonna do when winter ends. You're even making the movements with your arms, so you'll be a really renewed you.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You have to gather <radical>thread</radical> to <radical>sell</radical> if you want to <kanji>continue</kanji> living. You want to make a living, don't you? Make sure you continue to sell that thread every single day!
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine selling thread so you can continue living. You search for thread continually now. But are you gathering thread to continue to live, or living to continue gathering thread?
kanji.readingMnemonic: You have to <kanji>continue</kanji> to sell that thread because a Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang is forcing you to do it. You see? If you want to continue to live, you have to appease this Bosozoku gang. You better keep selling!
kanji.readingHint: Continue selling thread and paying off the Bosozoku. It's the only way you can continue to survive!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Your <radical>scooter</radical> and your <radical>car</radical> is what you always <kanji>take along</kanji> with you. Make sure they're <kanji>connect</kanji>ed well so you don't lose them!
kanji.meaningHint: Your scooter and your car, connected by a tiny little string, are the two things you take along with you wherever you go.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You <kanji>take along</kanji> your scooter and your car. Anything else, you can do a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん) for!
kanji.readingHint: The roof over your head? Rental. The clothes on your body? Rental. All of your friends? Rental. The only thing you permanently take along with you are your scooter and your car.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>sheep</radical> loses its <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> so it picks them up in its <radical>mouth</radical> to take them to someone who's <kanji>morally good</kanji> enough to attach them back onto its head. Sheep don’t have hands, so the sheep had to pick the horns up off the ground with his mouth. Someone evil would just murder the sheep and steal his horns, so the sheep must find someone <kanji>good</kanji> to reaffix them.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself as the good person being approached by this forlorn sheep without horns. Hear the clack of the horns together as the sheep drops them out of his mouth onto the ground and lets out a pathetic bleat. “Baa~aa~ah~”
kanji.readingMnemonic: Since you are <kanji>morally good</kanji>, you decide not to murder the sheep and harvest its organs. Instead, as a practitioner of the <kanji>good</kanji> teachings of <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) Buddhism, you decided to help the sheep out. Your Zen teachings have increased the power of your healing abilities, so you fix the sheep up in no time.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself using your Zen magic to heal this sheep. Place your hands on the sheep’s horns and imagine returning them to their rightful location on the sheep’s head. Feel the soft wool of the sheep as he bleats in happiness.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you put a <radical>thread</radical> in the middle of a <radical>kiss</radical>, you’re going to <kanji>entangle</kanji> it. Things can get pretty messy in a kiss, so adding a thread to the mix will definitely entangle it.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine trying to kiss someone with a piece of thread between your lips. Feel the thread getting all entangled between the two of you. Thread entangled, tongues entangled, lips entangled — just a thready kiss of entanglement.
kanji.readingMnemonic: This isn't going well, so you pull out the weird kiss thread and <kanji>entangle</kanji> it around a <reading>rack</reading> (らく). Good thing that rack was there! Much better to have it entangled around a rack when you're trying to kiss someone.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine pausing this kiss to pull a thread out of your mouth and entangle it around a random rack in your room. Smooth move, Casanova! Your partner will totally love that.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Within the <radical>bamboo</radical> grove, someone asks your <radical>reason</radical> for being there, and you explain it's to harvest bamboo to craft a <kanji>flute</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself cutting down some bamboo, thinking, "This is the reason I'm here, I need a flute, ASAP!" There is no other reason to be here besides this.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You cut down the bamboo and whittle a <kanji>flute</kanji>. You start playing a song. You know the reason why. An alien spacecraft comes flying down and drops a box full of alien <reading>tech</reading> (てき) in front of you.
kanji.readingHint: You've done what you came to do. You summoned an alien craft with your bamboo flute, gaining some alien tech in the process. Open up the box to see what tech awaits you.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>history</radical> radical and the <kanji>history</kanji> kanji are the same.
kanji.meaningHint: Radicals have a history of sometimes reappearing as kanji later on, don't they?
kanji.readingMnemonic: <kanji>History</kanji> was full of famous <reading>shee</reading>p (し). Sure, there were important people in history too, but historical sheep are where it's at.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine reading about sheep in a history book. There's Dolly, the first sheep clone in history, for example. And then there's… well, I'm sure there are tons.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you see some <radical>flowers</radical> that are <radical>dry</radical>, pull them up and you'll find a <kanji>potato</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Potatoes grow when flowers dry out. All the nutrients are going to the potato under the ground, so the flowers up top get sucked dry.
kanji.readingMnemonic: <kanji>Potato</kanji>es are totally <reading>emo</reading> (いも) because of the way they dry out flowers. Emo people and potatoes both like to dry out flowers.
kanji.readingHint: Picture an emo person sitting alone in a dark room, staring at a potato. "You and I are the same."

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>dirt</radical> next to your <radical>village</radical> is where you <kanji>bury</kanji> the dead. People don't live forever, so every village has a patch of dirt specifically used for burying dead things.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine the village where you grew up. Can you see the dirt area where all your ancestors were buried?
kanji.readingMnemonic: You don't just <kanji>bury</kanji> dead relatives here. You also buried your dead pet <reading>u</reading>ni (う) in that dirt. You thought having an uni as a pet would be a good idea. Too bad uni need water to survive.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine crying as you bury your pet uni. Your tears can't save it now. (Though it is a bit ironic, since it needed salt water.)

rawData.meaning_hint: Imagine the village where you grew up. Can you see the dirt area where all your ancestors were buried?

rawData.reading_mnemonic: You don't just <kanji>bury</kanji> dead relatives here. You also buried your dead pet <reading>u</reading>ni (う) in that dirt. You thought having an uni as a pet would be a good idea. Too bad uni need water to survive.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb.

You already learned 外す, which is used when YOU remove something. 外れる, on the other hand means <vocabulary>to come off</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to be off</vocabulary>. It's used when something ends up outside of where it's supposed to be — like a plug coming out of a socket. 

To remember the difference with 外す, just think of how <reading>rare</reading> (れる) it is for a plug to come out on its own, compared to when you intentionally remove it. It's kind of rare, right?

More figuratively, 外れる can also describe when your expectations are off — the results have ended up "outside" of what you were hoping for.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: When something is about <vocabulary>to come off</vocabulary>, you better hurry and put on your <reading>haz</reading>mat (はず) suit. Who knows what kind of toxic matter could spray out when this thing comes off. Better take all necessary precautions.

To remember the difference with 外す, just think of how <reading>rare</reading> (れる) it is for a plug to come out on its own, compared to when you intentionally remove it. It's kind of rare, right?

More figuratively, 外れる can also describe when your expectations are off — the results have ended up "outside" of what you were hoping for.

context_sentences.en: The wind was strong, and the arrow missed the target.
context_sentences.ja: 風が強くて矢がターゲットから外れてしまった。

context_sentences.en: I thought it would rain today, but my prediction was off.
context_sentences.ja: 今日は雨だと思ったのに、予想が外れた。

context_sentences.en: Since he came unplugged, Mr. Robot was no longer able to say, "thank you," anymore.
context_sentences.ja: プラグが外れたので、Mr.ロボットは「ありがとう」と言えなくなった。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you to remember it: 

Since you have generations of people making up this <vocabulary>world</vocabulary>, think of the rapper generation. What do they say all the time (obviously I don't know anything about rap)? They say "<reading>Yo</reading> yo yo!" (よ).

rawData.meaning_mnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
rawData.reading_mnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you to remember it: 

Since you have generations of people making up this <vocabulary>world</vocabulary>, think of the rapper generation. What do they say all the time (obviously I don't know anything about rap)? They say "<reading>Yo</reading> yo yo!" (よ).

context_sentences.en: This app still isn’t world famous.
context_sentences.ja: このアプリは、まだ世に出ていません。

context_sentences.en: There are all kinds of people in the world, right?
context_sentences.ja: 世の中には色々な人がいるもんですね。

context_sentences.en: In Japanese, "this world" refers to the current world while living, and "the other world" refers to the world after death.
context_sentences.ja: 日本語で「この世」とは生きている時の今の世界、「あの世」とは死後の世界のことです。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: 直り is the noun form of 直る, which you already know means "to be fixed." When a <kanji>relationship</kanji> is fixed, you get <vocabulary>reconciliation</vocabulary>. In other words, you're <vocabulary>making peace</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>making up</vocabulary> with someone. Good on you.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The readings are the kun'yomi readings. 仲 is the same as always, and 直り uses the reading from 直る. Nice and easy!

context_sentences.en: Let's make up.
context_sentences.ja: 仲直りしようよ。

context_sentences.en: Since I became an adult, I don't know how to make up.
context_sentences.ja: 大人になってから仲直りの仕方が分からなくなったんだ。

context_sentences.en: How did Koichi bring about a reconciliation between the Mario brothers?
context_sentences.ja: 一体全体、コウイチはどうやってマリオブラザーズを仲直りさせたの？

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with しい on the end, so you know it's an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>sad</kanji>? It's <vocabulary>sad</vocabulary>, sadly.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: You learned this reading with 悲しむ, hooray!

rawData.meaning_mnemonic: This is a single kanji with しい on the end, so you know it's an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>sad</kanji>? It's <vocabulary>sad</vocabulary>, sadly.

context_sentences.en: It's sad, but it can't be helped.
context_sentences.ja: 悲しいけど、しょうがないよ。

context_sentences.en: That's too sad.
context_sentences.ja: それは悲しすぎるね。

context_sentences.en: When you feel sad, Momo, neither your dad nor myself are happy.
context_sentences.ja: モモちゃんが悲しい気持ちの時は、お父さんもお母さんも悲しい気持ちになってしまうのよ。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>five</kanji> <kanji>feeling</kanji>s that you have are what? Sight, sound, taste, touch, and smell (don't count the sixth one where you see dead people). These are <vocabulary>the five senses</vocabulary> that you probably have.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

rawData.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: If you had to lose one of your five senses, which one would you give up? Please also tell me why.
context_sentences.ja: 五感のうちどれか一つを失ってしまうとしたら、どれを選びますか？また、その理由も教えてください。

context_sentences.en: The dishes at our restaurant are designed to be enjoyed with all five senses.
context_sentences.ja: うちのレストランの料理は五感で楽しめる料理となっています。

context_sentences.en: Using all five senses in one's everyday life is very important.
context_sentences.ja: 毎日、五感をしっかり使って生活することはとても大切なことです。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: When you're <kanji>request</kanji>ing <kanji>people</kanji>, you put up a <vocabulary>job posting</vocabulary> because you’re <vocabulary>seeking workers</vocabulary>.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

As you're looking at <vocabulary>job posting</vocabulary>s, you notice they're all looking for people with <reading>cu</reading>cumbers and <reading>jean</reading>s (きゅうじん). That's the only qualification they're after, cucumbers and jeans.

context_sentences.en: It looks like my brother's company is hiring.
context_sentences.ja: 兄の会社で求人してるみたいですよ。

context_sentences.en: Every day, I check online to see if there are any new job postings.
context_sentences.ja: 毎日新しい求人が出ていないか、オンラインでチェックしてます。

context_sentences.en: Ironically, this designer's job poster is not very well designed at all.
context_sentences.ja: このデザイナーの求人ポスターは、皮肉な事にデザインが全くダメだね。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: A big <kanji>mix</kanji> of folks forming a kind of <kanji>riot</kanji>. You'd think this would be something more violent, but it's really just an <vocabulary>orgy</vocabulary>. Lots of mixing, I guess, and not very much organization on the orgy-organizer's part, at least according to this kanji sequence.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: Her tongue lolled out and she passed out during the orgy.
context_sentences.ja: かの女は、乱交のと中で、舌をだらりと出して気ぜつしてしまったんです。

context_sentences.en: Hey, it's about time you quit it with the promiscuity, you've been putting everybody off.
context_sentences.ja: いい加減、乱交するのは止めなよ。みんなドン引きだよ。

context_sentences.en: "I look so good today, I may be asked to join an orgy." "Haha. You wish!"
context_sentences.ja: 「何か今日の俺、マジでイケてる。ひょっとしたらアイツらの乱交パーティにも誘われちゃうかも。」「ははは〜。そうだといいね。」

vocabulary.readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Make sure you remember ざい over さい, though. Why? Because you got all this debt trying to buy your way into <reading>Z</reading>ion.

rawData.meaning_mnemonic: The <kanji>wealth</kanji> you <kanji>borrow</kanji> is your <vocabulary>debt</vocabulary>.

context_sentences.en: It's not good to take out debt if you don't know how to pay it back.
context_sentences.ja: 返す目どが立っていないのに、借財をするのは良くないよ。

context_sentences.en: Is it true that Tofugu has a large amount of debt?
context_sentences.ja: トーフグが多がくの借財をかかえているというのは本当ですか？

context_sentences.en: After Fugu’s assassination, it became clear that the only thing he left behind for Salmon was debt.
context_sentences.ja: フグが暗殺された後、サーモンに残されたのは借財だけだということが明らかになった。
